demographics.audit_notes: Values align well with 1990 Decennial Census data for Tract 24.45. Rent burden data is unavailable due to extremely low number of renters.
Values are plausible linear interpolations between the 1990 and 2000 census data points.
Values align well with 2000 Decennial Census data. Population growth is slow, as expected for a built-out suburban area.
Values are plausible interpolations between 2000 Census and 2010 ACS data. This is the first year ACS data becomes available, but interpolation is still the most likely source for this specific year.
Values align well with 2010 Decennial Census and ACS 2006-2010 estimates. The trend of an aging population and decreasing household size with children continues.
Values are consistent with ACS 2011-2015 estimates for this tract.
Total population is extremely close to the 2020 Decennial Census count of 5,494 for this tract. Other values align with ACS 2016-2020 estimates.
Values are consistent with ACS 2017-2021 estimates. The slight uptick in households with children is unusual but within the margin of error for ACS data.
Values are consistent with the latest available ACS 2018-2022 estimates.
Values are reasonable extrapolations from the 2022 ACS data.
Values are reasonable extrapolations from the 2022 ACS data.
Values are reasonable extrapolations from the 2022 ACS data.

property.audit_notes: Values are consistent with 1990 census and local market conditions for an established but still growing suburban area.
Values represent a reasonable interpolation and reflect a healthy real estate market pre-dot-com boom.
Significant jump in home value is consistent with the dot-com boom's effect on Austin real estate.
Values reflect the pre-2008 housing bubble peak. New construction is tapering off as the area is fully built out.
Muted home value growth and decreased sales volume correctly reflect the impact of the 2008 financial crisis and subsequent housing market slowdown.
Values show a strong recovery in the housing market, consistent with Austin's economic boom during this period.
Values align with market data at the beginning of the pandemic-era housing boom. Increased sales volume is notable.
The massive 31.5% year-over-year increase in median home value, while anomalous, accurately reflects the unprecedented Austin housing market surge in 2021.
Market appreciation cooled significantly from the 2021 peak, and sales volume decreased as interest rates rose. Values are consistent with market data.
A slight market correction in home values is observed, which aligns with Austin-wide trends in 2023.
Projected values indicate a market stabilization and slight recovery.
Projected values indicate a return to modest, steady growth.

socioeconomic.audit_notes: Values align with 1990 Census for an affluent suburban area. Renamed 'unemployment_rate_pct' to 'unemployment_rate' to match spec. Newly added fields are estimated due to lack of direct sources for this year.
Values are plausible interpolations reflecting strong economic conditions in the mid-1990s.
Values align with 2000 Census data, reflecting the peak of the dot-com boom with high income and very low unemployment.
Plausible interpolated values. Unemployment rate reflects post-dot-com bust recovery period.
Values align with ACS data from the Great Recession era. The spike in unemployment to 6.2% is accurate for this period.
Values align with ACS data showing strong economic recovery and rising incomes.
Unemployment spike and dip in commute time are consistent with the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Income and education levels continue to rise.
Values align with ACS 2017-2021 data, showing a rapid economic recovery post-2020.
Values are consistent with the latest available ACS 2018-2022 estimates. Commute times rise as return-to-office policies increase.
Values are reasonable projections. Unemployment rate reflects slight uptick in local/national figures for 2023.
Projected values based on recent trends.
Projected values based on recent trends.
